food_subgroup: Noodle dishes with grilled meat and fried accompaniments

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese-style dry rice vermicelli with mixed grilled meats and crispy spring roll. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Pasta and noodles) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Vietnamese dry vermicelli with mixed grilled meats in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229961001 (Noodle dish) is the closest available parent concept. No specific code exists for this composite Vietnamese-style dish.

main_ingredients: sweet and sour fish sauce dressing

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of dry vermicelli with mixed grilled meats and crispy spring roll is approximately 450 g, comprising a base of rice vermicelli noodles topped with grilled pork, grilled chicken, nem nuong, a crispy spring roll, fresh herbs, pickled vegetables, crushed peanuts, and sweet-sour fish sauce dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Bun Bo Nam Bo (Dry Vietnamese Beef Noodle) and composite Vietnamese noodle dish estimates; FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — rice vermicelli, grilled pork, grilled chicken, spring roll components
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table; USDA Agricultural Research Service FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted averaging using: (1) FAO/INFOODS SEA values for rice vermicelli (cooked), grilled pork, and Vietnamese herb/vegetable components; (2) USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy values for grilled chicken breast, peanuts, and spring roll (fried). The composite per-100g profile reflects an approximate plate composition of ~40% cooked rice vermicelli, ~25% mixed grilled meats (pork, chicken, nem nuong), ~10% fried spring roll, ~15% vegetables/herbs/pickles, ~5% peanuts/scallion oil, ~5% fish sauce dressing. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli GI reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Rice vermicelli (cooked) GI ~53 (low). Composite dish GL estimated from serving carbohydrate content.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists for this exact composite dish. The GI of 53 is derived from the dominant carbohydrate source — cooked rice vermicelli — which has a published GI of approximately 53 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The glycemic load per serving is calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (53 × 88.3) / 100 ≈ 46.8, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 19.5 × 4.5 = 87.75 g ≈ 88.3 g. The composite dish contains protein and fat from grilled meats and spring roll which further attenuate the glycemic response, so GI 53 is a conservative upper estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce dressing and seasoned grilled meats
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,890 mg per standard 450 g serving), which approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced fish sauce or dressing on the side is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli base, partially offset by protein and fat from grilled meats
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard 450 g serving carries an estimated glycemic load of approximately 47, which is high; portion control is recommended, and the protein and fat content from grilled meats and peanuts will attenuate the postprandial glucose response compared with plain noodle dishes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from grilled pork and deep-fried spring roll, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation due to its saturated fat contribution from grilled pork and deep-fried spring roll (estimated 1.7 g saturated fat per 100 g) and its high sodium content; choosing smaller portions and requesting less dressing can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dry Vermicelli with Mixed Grilled Meat and Crispy Spring Roll is a Vietnamese-style noodle dish served at Co Chung Restaurant in Singapore. The dish consists of a base of cooked rice vermicelli (bún) served dry — without broth — topped with a combination of special grilled pork, grilled chicken, nem nuong (Vietnamese grilled pork sausage), mixed fresh vegetables, aromatic herbs, pickled vegetables, crushed peanuts, and scallion oil. A crispy deep-fried spring roll (chả giò) accompanies the dish. The defining element is a sweet and sour fish sauce dressing (nước chấm) that is poured over or served alongside the noodles, providing a balance of sweet, sour, salty, and umami flavours. Texturally, the dish combines the soft chewiness of rice vermicelli, the smoky char of grilled meats, the crunch of the spring roll and peanuts, and the freshness of herbs and pickles. Nutritionally, the dish provides a moderate energy density of approximately 168 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein from the grilled meats (approximately 10.2 g per 100 g) and moderate carbohydrate from the rice vermicelli base (approximately 19.5 g per 100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low) based on the rice vermicelli component, though the glycemic load per full serving is high due to portion size. Sodium content is elevated due to the fish sauce dressing, making this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with peanut or fish allergies. It is a popular Vietnamese-Singaporean restaurant offering that reflects the Vietnamese culinary diaspora in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted averaging using FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for the individual components (rice vermicelli, grilled pork, grilled chicken, spring roll, vegetables, peanuts, fish sauce). The GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source (rice vermicelli) using the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are approved and the estimation methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates; no exact match exists in any approved primary source for this composite dish. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy component sources for this dish type. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA component sources used for this estimation. The dish is labelled 'BTNĐB' in the source data, which likely refers to 'Bún Thịt Nướng Đặc Biệt' (Special Grilled Meat Vermicelli), a common Vietnamese restaurant designation. The Vietnamese local name and romanized alias reflect this interpretation. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains pork (grilled pork and nem nuong); it is not suitable for halal dietary requirements. The serving size of 450 g is an estimate based on typical restaurant portions for this dish category in Singapore; actual restaurant portion may vary. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from component fat profiles and should be treated as approximate.
